name: data-viz-bar-chart
description: Animated bar chart data visualization for 1920x1080, 8s duration. Five colored bars grow from a baseline with staggered easeOutExpo timing, value labels fade in after each bar reaches full height, a horizontal baseline rule, chart title, and Y-axis category labels. Corporate color palette. Use for infographic segments, broadcast data presentation, explainer videos, financial reports, and any quantitative data storytelling context.

design_notes.palette: Corporate Dark Stack §3.1. Background #0B0B0F, bars use 5 distinct colors from corporate palette: Bar 1: #3B82F6 (tech blue), Bar 2: #6366F1 (indigo), Bar 3: #22D3EE (cyan), Bar 4: #4C1D95-adjusted to #8B5CF6 (violet), Bar 5: #2D3748-adjusted to #64748B (slate). These 5 colors provide visual distinction while staying within the corporate blue-violet-slate family. White text for labels.
design_notes.layout: Chart occupies the center zone (x: 310–1610, y: 180–900). Title at top (y≈140). Baseline at y=860. Bars equally spaced: 5 bars in 1300px = 200px per bar with 60px gaps. Bar width 140px each. Bar heights proportional to data values (max bar reaches y=260 from baseline = 600px maximum height).
design_notes.easing: easeOutExpo (0.19, 1, 0.22, 1) for all bar growth — per §1.1: 'easeOutExpo — fastest deceleration, snappy/punchy motion design.' Bars grow from baseline with an explosive start that decelerates precisely at the target height. This reads as data 'filling' the bar with energy.
design_notes.stagger: 0.1s stagger between bars per the brief. Per §1.5: 'List items, cards: 2–3 frames @24fps = 83–125ms stagger — Tight, fast cascade.' 0.1s ≈ 3 frames @29.97fps — tight cascading bars that feel orchestrated.
design_notes.value_labels: Labels fade in 0.2s after each bar's primary growth completes. Per §1.3 Principle 7: 'Secondary action STARTS when primary action ENDS.' The label is secondary to the bar growth — it appears to 'pop' onto the chart after the bar settles.
design_notes.timing: Title entrance 0–0.5s, baseline draw-on 0.5–0.8s, bars stagger 0.8–2.5s (with easeOutExpo 0.6s per bar + 0.1s stagger), labels appear 1.0–2.7s (staggered). Hold 2.7–7.5s, exit begins 7.5s.

chart_title.description: Chart title displayed at the top of the frame.

bar_values.description: Data values as percentages (0–100) for each bar. These drive bar heights (100% = max height 600px from baseline). The 5 values map to bars 1–5 left to right.

bar_labels.description: Category label text below each bar. All-caps recommended for axis labels (§3.3 tracking rules apply).

value_label_prefix.description: Optional prefix for value labels (e.g. '$' for currency, '' for plain numbers).

value_label_suffix.description: Optional suffix for value labels. Default '%' for percentage data.

bar_colors.description: RGB 0–1 colors for bars 1–5. Defaults are the 5-color corporate palette from §3.1. Replace with brand-specific palette as needed.

steps.note: Background #0B0B0F = [0.043, 0.043, 0.059]. Corporate near-black from §3.1. Dark background ensures bars (with their distinct colors) pop with maximum contrast. This is the 'dark negative space makes bright elements pop harder' principle from §3.2.

Solid background layer. A dedicated solid (not just comp background) allows future effects or gradient overlays. Good production practice for data viz that may need background texture or color adjustments post-build.

Chart title at 54pt Bold. Per §3.3 type scale: 'Primary title: 60–90px, Bold — standard scene title.' Using 54px slightly under the range for a data viz title that co-exists with chart elements. Left-aligned to match the left axis — F-pattern reading: 'Primary horizontal scan across the top, vertical scan down the left edge' per §3.2. Color #F9FAFB warm white. Tracking +20 at subtitle size per §3.3.

Chart title at [310, 140]. x=310 aligns with the left edge of the chart area (96px outer margin + some breathing room). y=140 places it in the upper section, above the chart's 180px top boundary. This gives the title clear visual separation from the data.

Chart title fades in at the start, 0–0.5s. Title enters first, establishing context before the data animation begins. Per §1.3 Principle 3 Staging: 'Lead element animates first, supporting elements follow.' The title is the anchor — it answers 'what am I looking at?' before the bars answer 'here is the data.'
easeOut on title opacity entrance. Fast fade-in that decelerates to full opacity — standard professional entrance.

Horizontal baseline at the bottom of the chart. The baseline is the visual foundation of the bar chart — bars grow from it. A clear baseline is essential for data literacy: viewers need to know where 0 is. Per §3.2 composition: 'The eye anticipates motion: plant the next element there before it arrives.' The baseline appears before bars so viewers are oriented.
Shape group for baseline rectangle.

Baseline: 1300px wide × 2px tall at y=860. Width 1300px spans the chart area (x: 310–1610). Height 2px — thin line, clearly visible but not heavy. y=860 gives 220px of space below for category labels (bars grow upward from here). The 2px weight is the professional data viz standard — enough to register, too thin to dominate.

Baseline color #2D3748 = [0.176, 0.216, 0.282] — 'Border/Divider: Subtle separators' from §3.1 Corporate Dark Stack. A subtle gray-blue that reads as a structural line without competing with the colorful bars above it.

Baseline draws on from left to right: Scale X 0→100 while Y stays at 100, from t=0.5–0.8. 0.3s draw-on duration. The baseline appears after the chart title (t=0.5) and finishes before the first bar starts (t=0.8). This draw-on technique (per §2.1 Trim Paths / Scale-based wipe) creates a sense of the chart being constructed — a visual metaphor for data being revealed progressively.
easeOut on baseline draw-on. Starts fast (line shoots in from the left) and decelerates at the right endpoint. This is the forward momentum of chart construction.

Bar 1 shape layer. In a production implementation, this would be generated programmatically for N bars. Here we define 5 bars explicitly. Bar 1 represents the first data point (default: 72% height). Color: #3B82F6 tech blue from §3.1.
Shape group for Bar 1 rectangle.

Bar 1: 140px wide × 432px tall (72% of 600px max height = 432px). Position: x=450 = 310 (chart left) + 140px (bar width). y=644 places bar center so its bottom edge is at y=860 (baseline). Center y = baseline_y - (bar_height/2) = 860 - 216 = 644. Width 140px with 60px gaps between 5 bars: 5×140 + 4×60 = 700 + 240 = 940px... adjusted layout: bars start at x=380 with 200px center-to-center spacing.
Bar 1 color #3B82F6 = [0.231, 0.510, 0.965] — 'Accent (blue): Standard tech blue' from §3.1 Corporate Dark Stack. First bar gets the primary accent color, establishing it as the dominant data point.

Bar 1 position [450, 860] with anchor point at [0, 216] (bottom-center of the bar). The anchor at the bottom means Scale Y animation grows FROM the baseline upward. Without this, the bar would scale from its center — half would go above the data range, half below. Bottom-anchored scale is the correct data visualization technique for bars growing from a baseline.

Bar 1 scale: [100, 0] → [100, 100] from t=0.8–1.4 (0.6s duration). Scale X stays at 100 (bar width unchanged), Scale Y animates from 0 to 100 (grows vertically). 0.6s duration per bar: per §1.2 'Scale from 0 to 100% (snappy): 6–10 frames @24fps = 250–417ms' — our 0.6s (~18 frames @29.97fps) is slightly longer for 'weighted' feel. The first bar takes longest, subsequent bars use same duration for consistency.

easeOutExpo behavior on Bar 1 scale. Per §1.1 and the brief: 'Use easeOutExpo for bar growth.' easeOutExpo (0.19, 1, 0.22, 1) creates a snappy bar that shoots upward and decelerates sharply at the top — reading as data being 'poured' into the bar rapidly. The steep initial growth makes the chart feel active and energetic.
Bar 2: #6366F1 indigo. Default value 88% height = 528px. Centers at x=650 (200px right of Bar 1 center at x=450).
Shape group for Bar 2.
Bar 2: 140px wide × 528px tall (88% of 600px = 528px). y center = 860 - 264 = 596.

Bar 2 color #6366F1 = [0.388, 0.400, 0.945] — 'Accent (indigo): Modern SaaS accent' from §3.1 Corporate Dark Stack. Indigo provides clear differentiation from the blue of Bar 1 while remaining within the same cool-hue family.
Bar 2 bottom-anchored at [650, 860]. Anchor y=264 = half of bar height (528/2). Same bottom-anchor principle as Bar 1 for consistent growth-from-baseline behavior.

Bar 2 scale: grows from t=0.9–1.5 (0.1s stagger after Bar 1 which starts at t=0.8). Per brief: '0.1s stagger between bars'. Per §1.5: '2–3 frames stagger = 83–125ms — Tight, fast cascade.' 0.1s ≈ 3 frames @29.97fps. This tight stagger creates the cascade effect where bars appear to pop up in rapid sequence.
easeOutExpo on Bar 2. Consistent easing across all bars — they all use the same snappy growth curve, reinforcing that they're part of the same data series.
Bar 3: #22D3EE cyan. Default value 45% height = 270px. Centers at x=850.
Shape group for Bar 3.

Bar 3: 140px wide × 270px tall (45% of 600px = 270px). y center = 860 - 135 = 725. Bar 3 being the shortest at 45% creates visual contrast in the chart — the eye is drawn to the height differences. This variance communicates data variance and makes the chart readable.

Bar 3 color #22D3EE = [0.133, 0.827, 0.933] — cyan from §3.1 gradient combinations: 'Teal + Blue: #020617 → #1E40AF → #38BDF8 (Tech Blueprint).' Cyan provides the strongest hue shift in the palette — maximum differentiation from the blue/indigo of Bars 1–2. Per §3.1 'Pop accent: one contrasting hue against blues' guidance.
Bar 3 bottom-anchored at [850, 860]. Anchor y=135 = half of 270px.

Bar 3 grows from t=1.0–1.6. Staggered 0.1s after Bar 2 (t=0.9). The 0.1s/bar stagger means 5 bars span from t=0.8 to t=1.2 for start times, with last bar completing at t=1.8. Total bar animation phase: 0.8–1.8s ≈ 1s for the full cascade.
easeOutExpo on Bar 3. Uniform easing family across all bars.

Bar 4: #8B5CF6 violet. Default value 95% height = 570px. Centers at x=1050. The tallest bar at 95% — the visual peak of the chart. Per §3.2: 'Focal point should occupy 25–30% of total frame area.' A 570px tall bar at 140px wide = 79,800 sq px, while total frame = 2,073,600 sq px. At ~3.8% it's a focused element without overwhelming the composition.
Shape group for Bar 4.
Bar 4: 140px × 570px (95% of 600px = 570px). y center = 860 - 285 = 575. Tallest bar creates the visual climax of the chart animation sequence.

Bar 4 color #8B5CF6 = [0.545, 0.361, 0.965] — violet/purple from §3.1 'Corporate Slate Orchid: Pop #6366F1 (indigo)' palette. Slightly warmer than indigo (Bar 2), maintaining distinction. Violet on the tallest bar gives it visual authority — the peak bar has the most saturated/vivid color in the sequence.
Bar 4 bottom-anchored at [1050, 860]. Anchor y=285 = half of 570px.

Bar 4 grows from t=1.1–1.7. Staggered 0.1s after Bar 3 (t=1.0). At 95% height, this bar shoots up fast and high — the easeOutExpo makes it feel most energetic because it has the furthest distance to travel in the same time.
easeOutExpo on Bar 4.

Bar 5: #64748B slate. Default value 61% height = 366px. Centers at x=1250. Final bar in the sequence — uses a slightly muted slate color that de-emphasizes it relative to the preceding bars, creating a natural visual 'landing' after the peak of Bar 4.
Shape group for Bar 5.

Bar 5 color #64748B = [0.392, 0.455, 0.545] — slate gray from §3.1 'Border/Divider #2D3748... slightly lifted.' A cooler, less saturated hue for the final bar — it reads as distinct from the other 4 vivid colors while not dominating. Good for showing a contrasting data segment (e.g., a comparison baseline, a neutral month).
Bar 5 bottom-anchored at [1250, 860]. Anchor y=183 = half of 366px.
Bar 5 grows from t=1.2–1.8. Staggered 0.1s after Bar 4 (t=1.1). Final bar completes at t=1.8, closing the 5-bar cascade that started at t=0.8 (1.0s total cascade duration for 5 bars).
easeOutExpo on Bar 5. Completes the uniform easing across all 5 bars.

Value labels row. In production, each bar would ideally have its own individually-positioned text layer above it. This combined layer with manual letter-spacing represents the concept; adapt to 5 separate layers for precise placement. Font 28pt Bold — per §3.3: 'Body/supporting 18–26px, Regular' is too small for data labels; bold at 28px ensures legibility of critical numbers on-screen.

Value labels positioned at y=310, above the tallest bar's top (Bar 4 at 95% reaches y=860-570=290). Placing labels at y=310 keeps them 20px above the tallest bar, providing consistent clearance. Note: in production, each label would be positioned individually above its respective bar using the formula: y = 860 - bar_height - 20.

Value labels fade in at t=1.6–2.0 (0.4s after the last bar's growth begins at t=1.2). Per brief: 'Value labels above each bar that fade in after bar reaches full height.' Per §1.3 Principle 7: 'Secondary action STARTS when primary action ENDS.' Bar 4 (last before Bar 5) reaches height at t=1.7; labels appear at t=1.6 (slightly overlapping for fluid feel) and complete at t=2.0 (0.2s after all bars done). This is the 'secondary action within last 20% of primary' timing principle.
easeOut on value labels opacity. Labels appear quickly and settle to full visibility — matching the energy of the bars they annotate.

Category (axis) labels below baseline. Font 20pt Regular — per §3.3 'Caption/fine print: 16–20px, Light — absolute minimum legible.' Using Regular at 20px for axis labels. Color #94A3B8 = [0.580, 0.639, 0.722] — secondary/muted text from §3.1, subordinate to the value labels above bars. Tracking +150: all-caps labels at 20px per §3.3: 'Small labels/captions (18–30px): +75 to +150.' All-caps axis labels require aggressive tracking.

Category labels at y=900, 40px below the baseline at y=860. This 40px gap gives the labels breathing room from the baseline — the labels read as axis annotations, not as part of the bar structure. Per §3.3 leading: the 40px gap is approximately 2× the label height (20px) — standard caption clearance.

Category labels fade in at t=0.7–1.1 — appearing just before and during the bar animations (bars start at t=0.8). This slight early appearance is intentional: viewers need to see what category each bar represents BEFORE the bar grows, so they can track the data. Per §3.2: 'The eye anticipates motion — plant the next element there before it arrives.' Labels appear first, bars grow into them.
easeOut on category labels opacity entrance.
